Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd tell myself I was going to study for a few hours, but then I'd end up just kind of drifting through the material without really accomplishing much. I'm a hands-on learner, so sitting and reading or watching videos doesn't really stick for me. I'd often end up doing something more active, like working through problems or talking things out with classmates, but that wasn't always structured. Most weeks I'd put in around 20 hours, but the quality of that time varied a lot.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was coming up. I'd have these bursts of activity when something was due, but otherwise I'd kind of coast along. Some weeks I'd study at random times whenever I felt like it, other weeks I'd barely touch anything until the last minute. I never really established a consistent schedule even though I know that would probably help me. It's like I had the motivation somewhere inside but couldn't translate it into regular habits.
Q3: Usually I logged in because I saw a notification or remembered there was something I needed to check. Sometimes my peers would mention something in our group chat and I'd go see what they were talking about. I wasn't one of those people who logged in daily out of habit. It was more reactive than proactive, like I needed some external trigger to remind me the LMS existed.
Q4: There were brief periods where logging in felt more automatic, especially when I was actively participating in discussion posts with classmates. The peer support aspect actually helped because I'd want to see what others had said or respond to someone's question. But that routine never really stuck for the whole semester. It would come and go depending on how engaged the group was at any given time.
Q5: The discussion forums were actually pretty useful for me because I could interact with other students and work through ideas together. That social element made the learning feel more real and hands-on. The videos were okay for getting an overview, but I found myself zoning out after a while. I also liked when there were practice problems or interactive elements because just passively consuming content doesn't work well for how I learn.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the reading materials and supplementary resources. If it was just a PDF or a long article, I'd download it with good intentions and then never look at it again. The stuff that required me to just sit and absorb information passively felt like a waste of time because it wouldn't stick anyway. I also tended to skip optional quizzes unless someone told me they were actually helpful.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see an immediate connection to something practical or if it involved interacting with other people. I need to do something with the information, not just read about it. If I could discuss it with peers or apply it to a real problem, that made it feel meaningful. Otherwise, it just felt like going through the motions.
Q8: Optional activities felt like they existed in this gray zone where I knew they might help but couldn't justify spending time on them when required stuff was piling up. Required activities had this urgency that forced me to engage, even if reluctantly. With optional stuff, I'd think 'I'll get to that later' and later never came. The lack of external pressure meant my internal motivation wasn't enough to push me through.
Q9: Yeah, a lot of my actual studying happened outside the LMS. I'd download slides or PDFs and then work through them on my tablet while sitting somewhere comfortable. I also relied heavily on talking things through with classmates in person or over video calls. We'd work through problems together, and that collaborative learning was probably more valuable than anything I did alone on the platform.
Q10: There were definitely times when I was genuinely learning but the LMS wouldn't know about it. Like when I'd spend an hour discussing concepts with my study group or when I'd be working through problems on paper. I also learned a lot just from explaining things to others, which obviously doesn't show up anywhere. The LMS only sees clicks and logins, not the actual thinking happening.
Q11: All the time. I'd open a video once, download the transcript or slides, and then never watch the actual video again. I'd study from those downloaded materials on my own time, in my own way. Same with readings - I'd grab them and then read them on my phone during commutes or whatever. The LMS would just show one access, but I might have reviewed that material multiple times offline.
Q12: There was a stretch mid-semester where everything felt overwhelming even though my stress wasn't through the roof. I think I was just burnt out from juggling ten courses online. The content started blending together and I couldn't muster the energy to care about any particular assignment. That's when my assignment completion really dropped off, even though I was still showing up and logging in.
Q13: Definitely. When I had competing deadlines across multiple courses, I'd just do the bare minimum to not fail. Fatigue was a big factor too - after staring at screens all day, the last thing I wanted was more screen time. I'd log in, see everything I needed to do, feel overwhelmed, and then just close the tab. The workload across all my courses made it hard to give any single one my full attention.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd have a video playing while I was actually scrolling through my phone or thinking about something else entirely. Or I'd click through slides without really processing anything. I was technically 'engaged' according to the system, but my brain was somewhere else. It's easy to look active without actually learning anything.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly even when I knew they were important. Part of it was that I didn't feel confident I could do them well, so I'd avoid that discomfort. Part of it was just poor time management and thinking I'd have more energy or motivation later. I'd tell myself I work better under pressure, but really I was just avoiding the task until I had no choice.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. I'd have this pattern where I'd ignore something for weeks, then cram in the last day or two. My LMS activity would spike right before due dates and be pretty quiet otherwise. It's not a healthy way to learn, but that external pressure was the only thing that could cut through my tendency to drift.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it did affect me, but I often didn't engage with it as much as I should have. Positive feedback gave me a little boost, but I'd quickly forget about it. Critical feedback sometimes made me want to improve, but other times it just confirmed my feeling that I wasn't really cut out for this. The peer support helped more than instructor feedback honestly.
Q18: The course structure gave me a lot of flexibility, which sounds good but was actually kind of bad for someone like me. I need more external structure to stay on track. When things were spread out and self-paced, I'd drift and lose momentum. The discussion components helped because they created some social accountability, but overall I needed more scaffolding than the course provided.
Q19: Having everything online made it easier to access but also easier to ignore. I could always tell myself I'd do it later since it was always available. The discussion forums made engagement easier because I could see what peers were doing and feel some connection. But the lack of in-person, hands-on activities made it harder for me as a kinesthetic learner to really connect with the material.
Q20: That was literally me. I'd be in the LMS a lot, reading discussions, watching bits of videos, clicking around, but then not actually completing assignments. I think it's because browsing and exploring feels low-stakes, but submitting work feels high-stakes and permanent. You can engage with content without committing to being evaluated on it. Fear of failure probably plays into it too.
Q21: I can see how that happens. Some people are just efficient - they know what they need, they get it, and they leave. Or they're studying effectively offline using downloaded materials. I wasn't quite in that category since my exam score was just okay, but I definitely did more learning outside the system than inside it. The LMS activity doesn't equal learning.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active, how often you log in, what you click on. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement and everything that happens offline. They can't tell if you're actually thinking or just clicking. They miss conversations with classmates, reading printed notes, or that moment when something finally clicks while you're in the shower. It's a very incomplete picture.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting mental effort into understanding it. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials. For me, it involves actively working with the content - discussing it, applying it, questioning it. When I'm truly engaged, I lose track of time and want to know more. That happened occasionally this semester, but not consistently.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I relied on peer interactions outside the platform, or how my learning style made traditional online content less effective for me. It wouldn't capture my internal struggle between wanting to do well and feeling disconnected from the material. It also wouldn't show that I was juggling ten courses and spreading myself thin. The data shows what I did, not why I did it or what was going on in my head.
